title: Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Language Field Segmentation
problem: Current methods struggle to achieve fine-grained, open-vocabulary segmentation of 3D scenes using language queries without explicit region proposals, limiting their applicability in complex, real-world environments In particular, scalable 3D language field modeling remains difficult when queries must stay open-vocabulary and mask-free.
existing_methods: Existing approaches like Lerf and Weakly Supervised 3D OpenVocabulary Segmentation rely on CLIP embeddings and weakly supervised learning but lack hierarchical structuring for semantic coherence across volumes. Gaussian splatting provides efficient rendering but lacks integration with language-based segmentation.
motivation: The ability to segment 3D scenes based on open-vocabulary language queries is critical for applications such as augmented reality, robotics, and scene understanding, where precise semantic segmentation is required without prior object annotations This matters because 3D language field modeling should support efficient open-vocabulary interaction in real scenes.
hypothesis: A hierarchical framework that integrates CLIP embeddings with weakly supervised learning can effectively segment 3D scenes into semantically meaningful parts based on language input, improving both accuracy and coherence within a 3D language field.
method: We propose a hierarchical open-vocabulary language field segmentation framework that combines CLIP embeddings with weakly supervised learning. The method uses a multi-scale architecture to progressively refine segmentation results, leveraging the hierarchical structure of radiance fields to ensure semantic consistency across different levels of detail. Language queries are embedded and used to guide the segmentation process, enabling fine-grained object identification without explicit region proposals The resulting representation is optimized specifically as a 3D language field rather than a generic text-guided scene generator.
evaluation: Performance will be evaluated using segmentation accuracy and semantic coherence metrics on diverse 3D datasets. Baselines include existing methods like Lerf and Weakly Supervised 3D OpenVocabulary Segmentation. Evaluation will also consider computational efficiency and scalability across varying scene complexities.
significance: This approach advances 3D language field modeling by enabling more accurate and coherent open-vocabulary segmentation of complex 3D scenes, enhancing applications in AR, robotics, and scene understanding.
caveats: The method assumes access to high-quality CLIP embeddings and may face challenges in handling ambiguous or rare language queries. It also requires careful tuning of the hierarchical segmentation architecture to balance detail and computational cost.
